section.title: The Crucifixion and Burial
section.sectionRationale: The center of the Gospel: Jesus is crucified between two robbers, mocked as king, and cries out in God-forsaken darkness before yielding up His spirit. The temple veil tears, the earth quakes, a centurion confesses Him Son of God, and He is buried in a sealed, guarded tomb.
section.thesis: On the cross the King of the Jews bears the darkness of God-forsaken judgment for sinners; His death tears open the way to God, moves a pagan soldier to confess Him Son of God, and He is laid in a borrowed tomb, sealed and guarded, awaiting the third day.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the crucifixion and the mockery; the darkness, the cry of dereliction, and the death with its signs; the burial by Joseph of Arimathea; and the sealing of the tomb and the guard.

units.label: The crucifixion
units.note: the King of the Jews, mocked

verses.text: Along the way they found a man from Cyrene, named Simon, and they forced him to carry the cross of Jesus.
And when they came to a place called Golgotha, which means The Place of the Skull,
they offered Him wine to drink, mixed with gall; but after tasting it, He refused to drink it.
When they had crucified Him, they divided up His garments by casting lots.
And sitting down, they kept watch over Him there.
Above His head they posted the written charge against Him: THIS IS JESUS, THE KING OF THE JEWS.
Two robbers were crucified with Him, one on His right and the other on His left.
And those who passed by heaped abuse on Him, shaking their heads
and saying, “You who are going to destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days, save Yourself! If You are the Son of God, come down from the cross!”
In the same way, the chief priests, scribes, and elders mocked Him, saying,
“He saved others, but He cannot save Himself. He is the King of Israel! Let Him come down now from the cross, and we will believe in Him.
He trusts in God. Let God deliver Him now if He wants Him. For He said, ‘I am the Son of God.’”
In the same way, even the robbers who were crucified with Him berated Him.

units.label: The death of Jesus
units.note: darkness, the cry, and the torn veil

verses.text: From the sixth hour until the ninth hour darkness came over all the land.
About the ninth hour Jesus cried out in a loud voice, “Eli, Eli, lema {{sabachthani|sabachthani}}?” which means, “My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me?”
When some of those standing there heard this, they said, “He is calling Elijah.”
One of them quickly ran and brought a sponge. He filled it with sour wine, put it on a reed, and held it up for Jesus to drink.
But the others said, “Leave Him alone. Let us see if Elijah comes to save Him.”
When Jesus had cried out again in a loud voice, He yielded up His spirit.
At that moment the {{katapetasma|veil}} of the temple was torn in two from top to bottom. The earth quaked, and the rocks were split.
The tombs broke open, and the bodies of many saints who had fallen asleep were raised.
After Jesus’ resurrection, when they had come out of the tombs, they entered the holy city and appeared to many people.
When the centurion and those with him who were guarding Jesus saw the earthquake and all that had happened, they were terrified and said, “Truly this was the {{huios|Son}} of God.”
And many women were there, watching from a distance. They had followed Jesus from Galilee to minister to Him.
Among them were Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James and Joseph, and the mother of Zebedee’s sons.
When it was evening, there came a rich man from Arimathea named Joseph, who himself was a disciple of Jesus.
He went to Pilate to ask for the body of Jesus, and Pilate ordered that it be given to him.
So Joseph took the body, wrapped it in a clean linen cloth,
and placed it in his own new tomb that he had cut into the rock. Then he rolled a great stone across the entrance to the tomb and went away.
Mary Magdalene and the other Mary were sitting there opposite the tomb.
The next day, the one after Preparation Day, the chief priests and Pharisees assembled before Pilate.
“Sir,” they said, “we remember that while He was alive that deceiver said, ‘After three days I will rise again.’

So give the order that the tomb be secured until the third day. Otherwise, His disciples may come and steal Him away and tell the people He has risen from the dead. And this last deception would be worse than the first.”
“You have a guard,” Pilate said. “Go, make the tomb as secure as you know how.”
So they went and secured the tomb by sealing the stone and posting the guard.

sabachthani.gloss: You have forsaken Me. Part of Jesus' cry, Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani, quoting Psalm 22:1 in Aramaic. In the God-forsaken darkness Jesus bears the judgment due to sinners, yet the psalm He quotes moves from anguish to vindication and praise.

katapetasma.gloss: The curtain or veil that separated the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place in the temple, barring access to God's presence. At Jesus' death it is torn from top to bottom, God's own act, opening the way to God through the crucified Christ, Hebrews 10:19-20.

huios.gloss: Son. The pagan centurion, watching Jesus die, confesses truly this was the Son of God, the very title mocked at the cross; at the moment of apparent defeat a Gentile soldier proclaims the truth the whole Gospel has been unfolding.

basic.context: Simon of Cyrene is forced to carry Jesus' cross to Golgotha, where He is crucified between two robbers under the charge King of the Jews. Passersby, leaders, and even the robbers mock Him, taunting Him to save Himself. From noon darkness covers the land; at three Jesus cries out, My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me, and then yields up His spirit. The temple veil tears, the earth quakes, tombs open, and a centurion confesses, Truly this was the Son of God. Women watch from afar. Joseph of Arimathea buries Him in his new tomb, which the leaders seal and guard.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the heart of the Gospel, told with restraint and saturated with Psalm 22 and Isaiah 53. The mockery, save Yourself, unwittingly states the gospel: He could not save others and Himself. The cry of dereliction and the darkness signal that Jesus bears God's judgment. The torn veil interprets His death: access to God is opened. Read the signs, the veil, quake, and centurion, as God's own commentary.
basic.originalLanguages: In the darkness Jesus cries Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani, You have forsaken Me, bearing judgment for sinners. At His death the katapetasma, the veil, is torn open, and a Gentile confesses Him huios, the Son of God.
basic.typology: Jesus fulfills the suffering of Psalm 22, pierced, mocked, His garments divided, and the servant of Isaiah 53. The torn veil fulfills the whole temple system, opening the way into God's presence. The new tomb and the burial fulfill Isaiah 53's grave with the rich.
basic.christ: Jesus is the King reigning from the cross, the forsaken Son bearing the judgment of God, the true temple whose death opens access to God, and the Son of God confessed by a pagan soldier at the moment He dies.
basic.god: God is at work in the darkness: the Father does not spare His Son but gives Him up for us, and by tearing the veil He opens His own presence to sinners. The signs are His testimony that this death is the turning point of the world.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 22 is quoted and enacted throughout. Isaiah 53 gives the pierced servant buried with the rich. Hebrews 10 explains the torn veil as new access to God. Romans 8 declares God did not spare His own Son.
basic.oneStory: At the cross the whole story bends to its center: the King reigns from a tree, bearing the God-forsaken judgment sinners deserve, and His death tears open the way into God's presence that sin had closed since Eden. A Gentile confesses Him Son of God, a first fruit of the nations. He is buried, sealed, and guarded, and all creation holds its breath for the third day, when the story will turn from death to life.

context.literary: This is the climax of Matthew's passion narrative, structured around the mockery, the darkness and death, the accompanying signs, and the burial. The taunts echo the temptations of chapter 4 (if You are the Son of God) and the false temple charge. The tearing of the veil, the earthquake, and the centurion's confession form God's threefold answer to the mockery, and the sealed tomb sets up the resurrection.
context.historical: The condemned carried the crossbeam; Simon was pressed into service under Roman law. Golgotha lay outside the city wall. Wine mixed with gall was a bitter narcotic Jesus refused, meeting death with a clear mind. Crucifixion between robbers, the divided garments, and the placard naming the charge were standard Roman practice. The temple veil was a massive curtain. A rock-cut tomb sealed with a great stone and a Roman or temple guard made the site secure.
context.canonical: The scene fulfills Psalm 22 (forsakenness, mockery, divided garments, shaking heads) and Isaiah 53 (pierced, silent, buried with the rich). Amos 8:9 foretells darkness at noon. The torn veil is interpreted in Hebrews 9-10 as the new and living way into God's presence. The centurion's confession anticipates the Gentile mission of Matthew 28 and Acts.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present Jesus' death as the atoning, God-forsaken sacrifice of the true King and Son of God, foretold in Scripture, which opens access to God and draws even a Gentile to confess Him, and to establish the secured tomb as the setting for the resurrection.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The crucifixion narrative describes the central saving event of history rather than prescribing behavior. Its abiding call is to behold and trust the crucified Son of God, to confess Him as the centurion did, and to find in the torn veil the open way to God.

debates.question: What does Jesus' cry, My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me, mean?

views.label: A real bearing of God-forsaken judgment
views.case: In the darkness Jesus experiences the judicial abandonment due to sinners, drinking the cup of wrath; the cry expresses the genuine desolation of the sin-bearer separated from the Father's favor as He is made sin for us, 2 Corinthians 5:21.

views.label: A deliberate quotation of Psalm 22 that ends in vindication
views.case: By crying the opening line of Psalm 22, Jesus invokes the whole psalm, which moves from anguish through the very details being fulfilled to triumphant deliverance and worldwide worship; the cry voices real suffering yet points to certain vindication.

debates.bearing: Both hold together: Jesus truly bears the judgment and desolation of the cross, and in quoting Psalm 22 He entrusts Himself to the God who will vindicate Him, so that forsakenness and hope meet in the same cry.

study.originalLanguages: In the darkness Jesus cries out in Aramaic, Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani, You have forsaken Me, taking up Psalm 22 and bearing the God-forsaken judgment that sinners deserve. When He yields up His spirit, the katapetasma, the temple veil that barred the way into God's presence, is torn from top to bottom, an act of God Himself opening the Holy of Holies through the death of His Son. And at the very moment the mockers jeer at the Son of God, a Gentile centurion, terrified by the signs, confesses truly this was huios theou, the Son of God. The titles hurled in scorn are now spoken in awe, and the way to God stands open.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus mocked, His garments divided, His cry of forsakenness, all fulfilling Psalm 22:1, 7-8, 18.
The opened way into God's presence through Christ's flesh, Hebrews 10:19-20.
Jesus laid in the rich Joseph's new tomb, fulfilling Isaiah 53:9.

study.christ: Jesus reigns from the cross as the true King, the placard proclaiming the charge that is His glory: this is the King of the Jews. He is the Son of God who will not save Himself precisely because He is saving others, bearing in the darkness the God-forsaken judgment due to sinners and crying out the desolation of the sin-bearer. His death is no defeat but the tearing open of the way to God, for the veil is rent from top to bottom at the moment He dies. A pagan soldier, seeing how He dies, confesses Him Son of God, the first fruit of the nations who will be gathered. Laid in a borrowed tomb and sealed under guard, the crucified King awaits the vindication of the third day.
study.god: God is the hidden actor at the center of the cross. The Father does not spare His own Son but gives Him up for us all, and in the three hours of darkness the judgment against sin falls on the willing substitute. Then God Himself answers the mockery: He tears the veil from top to bottom, opening His once-barred presence to sinners; He shakes the earth and opens tombs as signs that a new age has dawned; and He draws a Gentile centurion to the truth. The very burial, in a rich man's tomb, and the sealing of the stone unfold under His sovereign hand, setting the stage for the resurrection by which He will vindicate His Son.

crossReferences.note: My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me, the cry Jesus takes on His lips, and the psalm fulfilled throughout the scene.
Assigned a grave with the wicked and with the rich in His death, fulfilled in Joseph's tomb.
Confidence to enter the Most Holy Place by a new and living way through the curtain, His flesh.
God did not spare His own Son but gave Him up for us all, the heart of the cross.

study.oneStory: At the cross the whole story bends to its center: the King reigns from a tree, bearing the God-forsaken judgment that sinners deserve, and His death tears open the way into God's presence that sin had closed since Eden. A Gentile confesses Him Son of God, a first fruit of the nations soon to be gathered. He is buried in a borrowed tomb, sealed and guarded, and creation holds its breath. The darkness of the cross is not the end; it is the price of the open door, and the sealed stone only sets the stage for the third day, when death itself will be undone.

quiz.q: What did the mockers unwittingly reveal when they said He saved others but cannot save Himself?

options.text: The truth of the gospel: to save others, He would not save Himself.
options.note: Their taunt states the very logic of the atonement, that He stayed on the cross to save sinners.

options.text: That Jesus had lost all His power on the cross.
options.note: He could have come down but chose not to; the irony is that His staying is His saving power, not weakness.

options.text: That Jesus had never really helped anyone.
options.note: They admit He saved others; the point is He would not spare Himself in order to save them.

options.text: That Jesus deserved to be on the cross.
options.note: He was innocent; the taunt ironically declares His saving purpose, not His guilt.

quiz.q: What is the significance of the temple veil tearing at Jesus' death?

options.text: God Himself opened the way into His presence through the death of His Son.
options.note: Torn from top to bottom, it signals new access to God, Hebrews 10:19-20.

options.text: The temple was damaged by the earthquake.
options.note: The quake accompanies it, but the tearing of the veil is a purposeful sign of opened access, not mere damage.

options.text: The temple was now to be abandoned by worshipers.
options.note: The point is opened access to God through Christ, not simply abandoning the building.
Matthew presents it as a weighty, God-given sign at the moment of Jesus' death.

quiz.q: Who confessed Jesus as the Son of God at His death, and why is it striking?

options.text: A pagan centurion, using the very title that had been mocked, moved by how Jesus died and the signs.
options.note: Verse 54 shows a Gentile soldier confessing the truth at the cross.

options.text: One of the Jewish leaders who had condemned Him.
options.note: It was a Gentile centurion, not a leader; that a pagan confessed Him is the striking point.

options.text: One of the robbers crucified beside Him.
options.note: In Matthew both robbers berated Him; the confession came from the centurion.

options.text: The crowd that had called for His crucifixion.
options.note: The crowd mocked; it was the centurion who confessed Him Son of God.

quiz.q: Why did the leaders ask Pilate to secure the tomb?

options.text: They remembered Jesus' promise to rise and wanted to prevent any claim of resurrection.
options.note: Verses 63-64 show they sealed and guarded the tomb against His rising, ironically strengthening the evidence.

options.text: They wanted to honor Jesus' body with proper protection.
options.note: Their concern was to prevent a resurrection claim, not to honor Him, which this misreads.

options.text: They feared the disciples would attack them.
options.note: Their stated fear was a stolen-body resurrection claim, not personal attack.

options.text: They believed Jesus would certainly rise again.
options.note: They called it a deception; they sought to prevent, not confirm, a resurrection.

questions: Jesus refused to come down from the cross so that He could save others. How does it change your view of His love to know He stayed there for you?
In the darkness Jesus was forsaken so that His people never will be. When you feel abandoned, how does His cry of dereliction speak to your own?
The veil was torn from top to bottom, opening the way to God. Are you living as someone with free access to God through Christ, or still standing at a distance?
A pagan soldier confessed Jesus as the Son of God by watching how He died. What about the cross most moves you to confess Him as Lord?
The tomb was sealed and guarded, humanly secure against any hope. Where do you need to trust God with a situation that looks sealed shut?

translationNotes.note: Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani is Aramaic (with a Hebrew form of the divine name), quoting Psalm 22:1; the bystanders' confusion with Elijah reflects the similar sound of Eli and Elijah.
The dividing of Jesus' garments by casting lots fulfills Psalm 22:18; some manuscripts add an explicit fulfillment citation, which the BSB places in a footnote.
Matthew alone records the opening of the tombs and the raising of many saints, who appear after Jesus' own resurrection; it is a sign that His death breaks the power of death for His people.
